entry.title: Graham Platner Denies Sex Assault Claim as Key Democrats Pull Endorsement
Social Security Proposal Would Raise Taxes for Millions to Save Program
Images Show Collapsed Roof, Submerged Cars as Heavy Rains Lash Northeast
Every Pesticide Approved Since the Supreme Court’s Roundup Ruling
DOGE Officially Ends: A Timeline of Cuts, Controversies
Medicine to Treat ‘Explosive’ Diarrhea Outbreaks Could Cause More Diarrhea
NASA’s Changing Stance on UFOs: Full Timeline
Republican Rebukes Trump Over ‘Foolish’ Greenland Comments at NATO
Map Shows States Where Trump Admin is Deploying Election Monitors
What Last Photo of Mitch McConnell Seen in Public Reveals
NPR Says Reporter Misheard Alito Retirement: ‘Profoundly Regret the Error’
entry.summary: A freight train derailment in Bucks County, Pennsylvania, on Tuesday prompted authorities to issue a shelter-in-place order for nearby residents as emergency crews rushed to assess the potential danger from overturned rail cars. The derailment occurred at approximately 1:59 p.m. ET and involved a CSX train, CSX media relations manager Jonathan Stuckey told Newsweek. Later in the afternoon, the Bensalem Police Department confirmed that no hazmat leaks had occurred during the derailment, despite previous concern that some of the derailed train cars were carrying hazardous materials. Evacuation orders issued for nearby homes and businesses have been lifted, ABC 6 reported. "A CSX train derailed multiple cars near the crossing of East Bristol Road and Grave Avenue in Trevose, Pennsylvania," Stuckey said. "Bucks County Emergency Management was immediately notified and is responding to the scene. There were no injuries reported. Our primary focus is on the safety of onsite personnel, the surrounding community, and the environment." According to Bensalem police, officers, firefighters, EMS personnel and hazardous materials teams responded to the scene between Street Road and the Neshaminy Falls train station after multiple freight cars derailed. Police initially reported that between five and 10 train cars had derailed and said authorities were working to determine what the train was carrying. Stuckey did not confirm if any hazardous materials were being transported by the train. Residents in the area were urged to remain indoors and avoid the scene while crews evaluated the risk. "CSX crews are currently on scene and working as safely and quickly as possible to restore the impacted site," Stuckey said. "The cause of the incident is currently under investigation. We will provide more information as it becomes available." A map of the incident places the derailment near the Neshaminy Falls station in Bensalem Township, northeast of Philadelphia. Video posted to social media showed numerous rail cars off the tracks. Train derailments involving freight cars can pose significant hazards, particularly when authorities do not yet know what a train is transporting. Even without a fire or visible spill, overturned cars may contain chemicals, fuels or other materials that could threaten nearby communities, waterways or air quality. That risk often leads officials to issue shelter-in-place advisories while hazmat teams identify the cargo and determine whether any hazardous substances have been released. Pennsylvania Senator John Fetterman, a Democrat, responded to news of the derailment on X: "Our team and I are tracking the train derailment in Bucks County. Those in the area should listen to local officials as more information becomes available. Keeping all of those affected in my thoughts right now." According to CSX''s 2024 Pennsylvania fact sheet, the railroad hauls a wide range of freight throughout the state. The largest commodity groups shipped by volume include: Pennsylvania is a major state for CSX, with more than 2,000 track miles and major facilities in the Pittsburgh and Philadelphia areas. The state serves as both an origin and a destination for local industries and a corridor for freight between East Coast ports and inland markets. Contact Newsweek editors on this story: Gabe Whisnant, Sam Wilson and Gray R. Thomas.

entry.title: Pennsylvania Train Derailment Map, Video as Police Warn of Hazmat Response
entry.summary: The global AI race is fueling a surge of data centers in the United States, where droves of lawmakers and municipalities are urging caution amid the construction boom. Legislators in at least 14 states nationwide are mulling bans or temporary moratoriums on new facilities, according to the National Conference of State Legislatures (NCSL). Critics of these facilities, which require large amounts of electricity and water to operate, insist that a cautious approach gives local leaders adequate time to assess long-term impacts, while proponents, including the Trump administration, believe data centers are critical to defeating China in the AI dominance race. Here''s a closer look at the 14 states considering moratoriums, as well as local municipalities that have already enacted bans, including Monterey Park, California, joining other municipalities throughout Georgia and Indiana with existing restrictions. The ongoing efforts to limit the proliferation of data centers include proposals to block their construction throughout Georgia through 2028 and a moratorium on larger facilities in Maine until late 2027, which was recently vetoed, according to NCSL data. Other similar attempts have failed thus far in Oklahoma, Minnesota and New Hampshire, among other states. Measures have also been introduced in South Carolina seeking to forbid local approval of data centers until legislators adopt a comprehensive oversight framework in the state, while lawmakers in Pennsylvania want a three-year moratorium on new data centers to be accompanied by impact studies, according to NCSL, which tracks the efforts. More than 3,000 data centers are already operating throughout the United States, with more than 1,500 new facilities in various stages of construction, according to the Pew Research Center. The majority of the planned data centers are located in rural areas, particularly through the South and Midwest, while 87 percent of existing facilities are housed in urban settings, according to the Washington-based think tank. As of April, Virginia and Texas had the most operating data centers in the U.S., followed by California, Ohio and New York, Pew researchers found. Several proposed or under-construction facilities are located directly above or adjacent to the Ogallala Aquifer, a groundwater system that underpins much of America''s agricultural production. Construction at a proposed 438-acre site in Texas near Odessa is expected to begin next year, while Microsoft announced plans in April to buy roughly 3,200 additional acres in Wyoming to expand data centers there. Other major proposed data centers near the aquifer include facilities in Natrona County, Wyoming and in San Angelo, Texas. The Ogallala Aquifer, which spans beneath eight states, remains one of the most important groundwater resources in North America. Water losses in some subregions have been sustained for decades, with depletion rates "reaching over 100,000 acre-feet per year and nearly 27 percent of stored water already gone in some places," researchers previously said. "Unlike surface reservoirs, the aquifer recharges extremely slowly," Colorado State University electrical and computer engineering professor Sudeep Pasricha told Newsweek on Sunday, adding that recharge rates in parts of the Great Plains often total less than an inch per year, far less than the amount taken for irrigation. That lost water could take "centuries to millennia in some areas" to return, Pasricha said. Contact Newsweek editors on this story: Jenni Fink and Sam Wilson.

entry.title: Map Shows Where Data Centers Are Being Banned
entry.summary: The U.S. Supreme Court on Tuesday set up another major Second Amendment ruling that could have ripple effects across multiple states which have restrictions on semi-automatic weapons. The cases—out of Chicago and Connecticut—test a core unresolved question in modern gun law: whether firearms like AR‑15‑style rifles are protected because they are widely owned for lawful purposes or can be banned because governments view them as especially dangerous. The court said it would hear oral arguments during the fall. A ruling could affect assault‑weapons bans in roughly a dozen states and major cities, and either reinforce or significantly expand the Court’s recent trajectory on gun rights. “Connecticut’s assault weapon ban is lawful, lifesaving, and broadly supported. The gun lobby has flooded the courts in states across the country to get an assault weapons case up to this Supreme Court," Connecticut Attorney General William Tong said in a statement shared with Newsweek. "We are prepared for this fight, and we are going to go in with everything we’ve got to keep these weapons of war off our streets, out of our schools, and away from our families." The Illinois case centers on a Cook County ordinance that bans certain semiautomatic rifles, including AR‑15‑style weapons, and prohibits their possession, sale and transfer. Plaintiffs argue the law violates the Second Amendment because the weapons are in “common use” by Americans for lawful purposes, such as self‑defense. County officials, meanwhile, defend the ban as a public‑safety measure aimed at reducing the risk of mass shootings and limiting access to high‑powered weapons. The Connecticut case challenges a statewide assault‑weapons ban enacted after the 2012 Sandy Hook school shooting. That law restricts possession of certain semiautomatic rifles unless they were owned before a 2013 cutoff and registered with the state and defines banned weapons by name and specific features. State officials opposed the Supreme Court taking up the case, arguing that only a small fraction of the population owns semiautomatic weapons. "More importantly, Petitioners’ evidence did not address the second question of why Americans choose to own assault weapons, much less show that Americans subjectively choose them for self-defense," their brief filed to the court read. "Nor did Petitioners submit any evidence showing these weapons are appropriate or ever used for self-defense in practice." The court’s decision to take the two cases comes after years of conflicting rulings in lower courts applying the Supreme Court’s 2022 decision in New York State Rifle & Pistol Association v. Bruen. That ruling required judges to evaluate gun restrictions based on whether they are consistent with the nation’s historical tradition of firearm regulation. Since Bruen, federal appeals courts have split on how to apply that test to assault‑weapons bans. Some courts, including the Seventh and Second Circuits, have upheld such bans, while gun rights advocates argue those rulings misapply the Supreme Court’s framework and allow governments to prohibit weapons that are commonly owned. The justices appear to have been watching the issue develop before stepping in. Both petitions were distributed for conference repeatedly over months, a sign the Court was weighing whether to take up the issue or wait for additional cases. Now, by granting review and consolidating the cases, the Court is poised to issue what could be its most consequential Second Amendment ruling since Bruen—one that could determine whether bans on semiautomatic rifles remain legal nationwide. The ruling could have sweeping implications for state laws across the country, despite them not being included in the two specific cases. Roughly a dozen states and the District of Columbia currently have some form of assault‑weapons restriction. These include California, Colorado, Connecticut, Delaware, Hawaii''s partial ban on assault pistols, Illinois, Maryland, Massachusetts, New Jersey, New York, Rhode Island and Washington, as well as Washington, D.C. While the details vary significantly, most of these laws bar the sale, possession or transfer of certain semiautomatic firearms defined by features such as detachable magazines and military‑style characteristics. Some states include grandfather clauses for previously owned weapons, while others impose registration requirements or limits tied to magazine capacity. The Supreme Court’s eventual decision could determine whether those types of bans—many of which have been in place for years or enacted after high‑profile shootings—can continue to be enforced nationwide. In Viramontes v. Cook County, the challengers include Cutberto Viramontes and other individuals, along with gun‑rights groups such as the Firearms Policy Coalition and the Second Amendment Foundation. They are opposing Cook County, Illinois, and local officials including County Board President Toni Preckwinkle and State’s Attorney Kimberly Foxx. "This is not the end of the fight to eliminate bans on so-called ‘assault weapons,’ but it marks the end of the beginning," FPC said in a statement Tuesday. "For years, the FPC Grassroots Army has fought hard to bring this question to the Supreme Court, and now the time has come to march forward and reclaim the rights that were immorally taken from us."

entry.title: Map Shows Where AR-15 Bans Could Face Supreme Court Fallout
entry.summary: A developing area of low pressure off the southeastern U.S. coast could become Tropical Storm Bertha as it drifts toward Florida later this week, with forecasters watching a narrow window for tropical development. The National Hurricane Center''s (NHC) latest Tropical Weather Outlook said the disturbance, located offshore of the southeastern U.S. coast, remains poorly organized and has only a 10 percent chance of tropical formation during the next 48 hours. Forecasters said that environmental conditions are becoming less favorable for significant strengthening as the system approaches Florida. Even so, if the circulation becomes sufficiently organized and receives a name, it would be Bertha, the second named storm of the 2026 Atlantic hurricane season. The NHC said Tuesday that the disturbance was moving southward and is expected to turn westward later this week, while AccuWeather Lead Hurricane Expert Alex DaSilva said any tropical development would most likely occur on Wednesday or Thursday, making the next 48 hours critical in determining whether the system becomes a tropical depression or tropical storm before reaching Florida. If it forms, Bertha would be only the second named storm of what the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) expects to be a relatively quiet Atlantic hurricane season. Earlier this year, the agency forecast eight to 14 named storms, including three to six hurricanes and one to three major hurricanes, while emphasizing that even a below-average season can produce destructive landfalls. NOAA said there is a 55 percent chance of a below-normal season, largely because developing El Niño conditions are expected to increase wind shear over the Atlantic and make it harder for tropical systems to organize. Hurricanes need a relatively calm, vertically aligned atmosphere to develop and strengthen, but stronger upper-level winds associated with El Niño can disrupt thunderstorms, tilt developing storms, and make it harder for tropical cyclones to organize into hurricanes. As a result, El Niño years often see fewer Atlantic storms, hurricanes and major hurricanes than average. In the case of the developing disturbance in the Atlantic, DaSilva told Newsweek that wind shear isn''t very strong. "There''s not a lot of wind shear, but dry air attached to the cold front will prevent any kind of development," DaSilva said. Meanwhile, a heat dome bringing oppressive temperatures to millions across the Midwest and East Coast could be forcing dry air into the system and "squashing it down," DaSilva said. The disturbance is expected to move near the Gulf Stream over the next few days, and if a tropical depression were to develop, this is where it would occur, DaSilva said. "We still do need to keep an eye on it. We''ve seen storms play games off the East Coast and get a little energy and boom, they could become a tropical depression or something," DaSilva said, adding that AccuWeather "can''t rule out" potential development. If a tropical depression forms, it will do so on July 1 or 2, when the disturbance moves over the warmer water of the Gulf Stream. However, there is not much moisture associated with the disturbance, so the greatest risks will be increased rip currents from Florida''s eastern coast up through the Carolinas. The enhanced surf will begin as early as Tuesday afternoon. As people flock to the beaches to cool off during the heat wave, DaSilva warned them to practice ocean safety, given the elevated rip current risk. In addition to the disturbance in the Atlantic, the NHC also is tracking a disturbance in the Pacific Ocean with an 80 percent chance of development in the next 48 hours.

entry.title: Potential Tropical Storm Bertha: Timeline as System Nears Florida
entry.summary: The potency of the U.S. passport has declined significantly from its recent highs, and the country has failed to crack the top 10 in a recent ranking of the world''s most powerful passports. According to the annual Global Passport Report from Global Citizen Solutions, a mobility firm specializing in residency and citizenship planning, the U.S. now ranks 12th in terms of overall strength—based on a composite assessment of mobility, investment attractiveness and the quality of life enjoyed by those living there. It marks a slight improvement from 2025—when it was ranked 14th—and the firm noted that the U.S. passport remains “one of the most desirable" in the world. But the U.S. has fallen from first place in only five years, which the firm described as “the steepest five-year decline of any G7 country and one of the most significant trajectory reversals in the dataset for a major economy.” According to Global Citizen Solutions, the U.S. presents the report’s “most significant case study in structural decline.” In 2021, the U.S. achieved the highest ever composite score of 96.45, which has now fallen to 92.37 in large part due to its failure to keep up in terms of “mobility.” The category—which accounts for 50 percent of the overall score—gauges the travel freedoms granted by a certain passport as well as whether that country is a “desirable” destination. The U.S. mobility rank fell from 10th place in 2021 to 41st in 2026. And despite its citizens enjoying visa-free access to 110 countries, the U.S. remains the “10th most restrictive destination in the world,” with visa waiver programs covering only 43 of 197 nations. The “most visible catalyst” of the mobility decline, researchers said, was Brazil reinstating visa requirements for Americans last April, after the U.S. failed to reciprocate the country waiving them from 2019 onward. China has also expanded visa-free entry to many nations—though not the United States—in recent years, they said, and the rollout of new European Union entry procedures have added “procedural friction that did not exist in 2021.” "The American passport is a story of diverging metrics,” said Patricia Casaburi, CEO of Global Citizen Solutions. “On raw travel freedom it has fallen from 10th in the world in 2021 to 41st in 2026, a 31-place slide that reflects how aggressively other nations have expanded visa-free access while the U.S. has stood still, and in some cases lost ground, as countries such as Brazil reinstated visa requirements for American travelers.” In 12th place, the U.S. is tied with France but behind many G7 peers. The top 10, according to Tuesday’s report: In separate rankings published by the British investment migration consultancy Henley & Partners, the U.S. passport currently sits in 10th place, tied with Iceland. That marks an improvement from its 2025 ranking of 12th—the first time in the index’s 20-year history that the U.S. placed outside the top 10.

entry.title: UK Finding Copy of US Declaration of Independence ‘Astonishing’—Historian
entry.summary: A volunteer at the British National Archives uncovered a new copy of the Declaration of Independence ahead of the United States’ 250th anniversary. The newly uncovered document is one of the rarest forms of the Declaration, printed in Exeter, New Hampshire, following its adoption. Ten of the surviving copies are held in U.S. libraries and historical societies, but this one offers a unique international link to America’s founding. The discovery was announced as millions of Americans plan to celebrate the semiquincentennial over the weekend. Emily Sneff, a historian and author of When the Declaration of Independence Was News, told Newsweek the discovery is “astonishing” in a phone interview on Friday. “The fact that it has survived for 250 years is astonishing,” she said. “Every copy of the Declaration that we have today is evidence of someone thinking ahead and preserving it, folding it up, making sure it would last for generations.” The copy was found by Michael Scurr, a volunteer on the archives’ America 250 – Royal Navy Admirals’ and Captains’ letters project. He found the copy while cataloging a box of letters, among letters sent by HMS Raisonable Captain Thomas Fitzherbert, according to the British National Archives. Scurr told the Associated Press that when he found the copy, he thought, "Oh, right, OK, this is definitely a Declaration of Independence. How exciting is this?’’ The copy was taken from the papers of an American vessel called the Dalton, based out of Newburyport, Massachusetts. The ship was captured by the British on December 24, 1776. In a letter dated January 10, 1777, Captain Fitzherbert made a reference to documents seized from the Dalton, including a copy of the Exeter Declaration. The Exeter Declaration broadsides were printed in Exeter by Robert Luis Fowle, printer of the New Hampshire Gazette or Exeter Morning Chronicle, between July 16 and 19, 1776, just days after the 13 colonies declared independence from Great Britain, according to the archives. Prior to the discovery, only 10 copies of the broadsides were known to have survived. This is because these printings were typically “just meant for the immediate moments,” Sneff told Newsweek. “These copies of the Declaration created in the period of time when it was news were not necessarily meant to survive,” she said. “In this case, the New Hampshire broadsides were printed on rag-made paper, not the highest quality of paper, you can see the flecks of different colors of textiles within the broadside paper.” At the time, the Declaration was printed in newspapers, and there were public readings of it that relied on these broadsides, Sneff said. These copies were mostly intended for local consumption and would have been distributed around the Exeter region. Copies that ended up in the British National Archives were mostly ones that fell into the hands of British officers who were in the vicinity of a particular printing, she said. “In this case it seems as though these Exeter, New Hampshire, broadsides were intended for a pretty local audience, and it just happens that it was a local ship that months later ended up in this situation of interception,” she said. Eric Slauter, deputy dean of arts and humanities at the University of Chicago, told Newsweek that single-sheet printings like this copy were how many Americans received news that the U.S. had declared independence from Great Britain. “American printers, beginning with John Dunlap in Philadelphia on July 4, 1776, were always careful to print the most important words Congress had declared in capital letters, as the printer of the Exeter, NH Declaration has done. We think Robert Fowle made his version of the Declaration in mid-July, as the news reached New Hampshire,” he said. It is “fitting” that it was seized in international waters, as the Declaration was an “instrument of international law,” Slauter said. “Because the philosophy in the second paragraph of the Declaration has been so central to American identity as a charter of liberty, equality, and rights, we tend, mistakenly, to think of the Declaration exclusively in terms of domestic politics,” he said. “But when Thomas Paine pushed in Common Sense in January 1776 for a declaration of independence he described it as a ‘manifesto’ that should be dispatched to European courts, with the hope of securing a military alliance and commerce.” There is only one copy of the engrossed, signed Declaration of Independence, and it is housed in the National Archives in Washington, D.C., according to Harvard University’s Declaration Resources Project. However, publishers produced hundreds of copies of the Declaration after its adoption. Philadelphia printer John Dunlap was the first printer to make a broadside copy of the Declaration and went on to print more than 200 copies. Here are the locations of those broadsides, per Harvard:

entry.title: The AI Squeeze Is Showing Up in Jobs Figures
entry.summary: The impact of artificial intelligence on the size and composition of the American workforce has long been a subject of discussion for economists and lawmakers, and signs of this impact have now begun to emerge in monthly jobs figures. On Thursday, the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) revealed that the economy added 57,000 jobs in June, around half the forecast amount, bringing an end to a series of better-than-expected employment readings. Fewer gains than previous months were recorded across several sectors, while many others saw declines or ongoing stagnation. This includes financial-activities and information—two of the sectors that have taken the lead in adopting and promoting the use of AI in their industries. This comes a day after a report from the outplacement firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas revealed that AI continues to be the most cited reason behind layoff announcements in 2026. Combined with downward revisions to previous months’ figures, the latest employment report was viewed as underwhelming by most analysts. Daniel Zhao, chief economist at Glassdoor, told Newsweek on Thursday that this pointed to a "hiring slowdown" which "runs deeper than the headline numbers first let on." "The unemployment rate''s decline to 4.2 percent is a case of good news for the wrong reasons: it was driven by people leaving the labor force, not by more hiring," he added. "This points to a labor market that''s stubbornly refusing to reaccelerate, despite recent optimism." According to the BLS, the financial activities and information sectors have together shed around 150,000 roles—25,000 per month on average—in 2026. And these two sectors were among those identified as leading the charge in AI usage in a recent report from Goldman Sachs, quoted by MarketWatch, alongside professional services and education firms. Overall, researchers found that AI adoption among American firms had grown to 20.6 percent—up from 19.5 percent in May—while noting that the employment impacts of this had so far been offset by a construction sector boost as "as tech companies race to build data centers." And while they said that there was not yet a "statistically significant" connection between the technology and unemployment figures, Challenger, Gray & Christmas’s latest report found that AI was again the "dominant force" in June’s layoff announcements. AI led all reasons for cuts last month at 14,029 (31 percent of the total) and has been cited in 101,743 announcements this year. "Rapid improvements in AI capabilities and growing corporate efforts to integrate the technology into enterprise workflows have raised concerns that AI could trigger a ‘job apocalypse,’" Goldman Sachs researchers wrote in a recent report, "especially as some companies have cited AI as a factor in recent layoffs." Many tech giants, such as Meta and Microsoft, have cited their increased focus on AI when announcing large-scale reductions this year, as economists continue to warn that millions of jobs are vulnerable to replacement by the technology. Some have suggested that these announcements could be a case of "AI-washing"—with companies overstating the impact of AI on layoffs, rather than blaming structural factors like over-hiring or reduced margins—and others have said the impact on employment in the U.S. could be more complex than a simple reduction in human roles. Experts previously told Newsweek that AI could create many new employment opportunities, and Kevin Buehler, CEO of the financial AI platform Rogo, said that many incorrectly believe that "if one job gets replaced by AI, it disappears." "That’s not quite what’s happening," he said at Newsweek’s AI Impact Forum in June. "what we see is other opportunities arise across the economy."

entry.title: List of Slavery Signs Trump Can Remove From National Parks After Court Win
entry.summary: A federal appeals court has handed the Trump administration a significant, if temporary, legal victory in its effort to reshape how science and American history are presented across national parks—pausing a lower court ruling that had ordered the government to restore hundreds of removed exhibits covering topics such as slavery, civil rights and climate change. The U.S. Court of Appeals for the First Circuit temporarily halted the lower court order on July 2, which would have forced the National Park Service (NPS) to reinstall removed or altered displays by the following day. District Judge Angel Kelley in Boston ruled in June that the removal of these exhibits set "a dangerous precedent of censorship and sanitization." The development reflects the continued conflict over the Trump administration''s executive order, titled Restoring Truth and Sanity to American History, and Secretary''s Order 3431, which prompted the Department of the Interior to review and remove a wide range of exhibits in the country''s national parks based on what the department determined was "concerning" content. Following the removal of certain exhibits, a coalition of organizations, represented by the nonprofit public policy research organization Democracy Forward, filed for a preliminary injunction to halt and reverse the "censorship" in national parks across the country. Brooke Menschel, the senior counsel at Democracy Forward, said in a statement shared with Newsweek following the court''s order that the organization was "disappointed by this decision," but that it saw the ruling as "merely a temporary procedural setback." Menschel continued, "The First Circuit did not condone the Trump-Vance administration''s censorship or issue any ruling on whether its actions are lawful." She added that it was unfortunate that the administration could continue removing or altering exhibits in the country''s national parks while legal proceedings unfolded. "Our national parks are places of learning, reflection, and truth—not political messaging, but the administration has politicized them through censorship," Menschel said. "We remain committed to defending the integrity of these treasured public spaces and ensuring they continue to work to tell the full American story, guided by history and the law." A spokesperson for the Interior Department told Newsweek that through the president, the department has "encouraged Americans to visit our cultural and historic sites and engage in meaningful conversations about the moments that have shaped our country." "By telling the full story, every triumph, every challenge, and every step toward a more perfect union we strengthen our shared understanding and ensure that future generations inherit not just the land we love, but the truth of the journey that brought us here," the spokesperson added. With the universal injunction paused, NPS is legally authorized to continue reviewing and permanently taking down displays it feels do not conform with the administration''s criteria. In early 2026, the Interior Department removed or flagged hundreds of items addressing slavery, abolition, civil rights, labor, immigration and women''s suffrage. The following flagged displays are now potentially subject to removal or alteration, according to a list provided by Democracy Forward: In an order issued on Thursday, a three-judge panel for the U.S. Court of Appeals for the First Circuit granted the Interior Department a stay order, pausing legal proceedings while the administration pursues an appeal. The ruling permits the agency to continue enforcing Secretary''s Order 3431 and suspends the immediate deadline to restore previously removed park exhibits. The court''s order overturns the immediate effects of a sweeping ruling Kelley had issued weeks earlier, which granted a preliminary injunction directing the Interior Department and NPS to reinstate historical and scientific materials removed under Trump''s executive order. In that decision, Kelley agreed with the plaintiff coalition that the administration''s censorship campaign likely violated the Administrative Procedure Act, the National Park Service Organic Act and the NPS Centennial Act. The judge declared that systematically stripping parks of their historical context constituted a "government-sanctioned erasure and rejection of [people''s] histories." She added that the move "degrades the public''s trust in the government." Contact Newsweek editors on this story: Matthew Robinson and Shakeema Edwards.

entry.title: The Number To Beat if July 4 Fireworks Are To Break a World Record
entry.summary: President Donald Trump''s planned July 4 fireworks spectacular is expected to feature more than 850,000 pyrotechnic effects—enough to surpass the current Guinness World Record if the display is successful. Freedom 250, the nonprofit helping organize America''s 250th birthday celebrations, has described the Washington, D.C., event as a "record-setting fireworks display" and says more than 850,000 pyrotechnic effects will be launched around the National Mall and Potomac River during a 35-minute finale to its Salute to America celebration. The display has the potential to beat the record set in the Philippines on New Year''s Day 2016 that has stood for more than a decade. According to Guinness World Records, the largest fireworks display ever recorded consisted of 810,904 fireworks launched by Iglesia Ni Cristo (Church of Christ) during its Countdown to 2016 celebrations at Ciudad de Victoria in Bocaue, Bulacan, Philippines. The display began at midnight on January 1, 2016, and lasted 1 hour, 1 minute and 32 seconds, with organizers successfully detonating enough fireworks to establish the current record despite heavy rain. Guinness rules state that the record is measured by the number of fireworks successfully detonated during a public display. Freedom 250 says the July 4 display will involve more than 850,000 pyrotechnic effects, launched from multiple locations around the National Mall, West Potomac Park and the Potomac River. Organizers say the show will last around 35 minutes and be roughly twice as long as a traditional National Mall fireworks display. If the final verified count exceeds 810,904 and satisfies Guinness requirements, the display would surpass the current record by nearly 40,000 fireworks. One thing worth noting is that Freedom 250 refers to "pyrotechnic effects," while Guinness measures the number of fireworks successfully detonated. Whether all effects count toward a record total would likely depend on Guinness'' verification process. The planned show would also be more concentrated than the current record holder. Based on Freedom 250''s published figures, more than 850,000 effects over 35 minutes would equate to roughly 24,000 effects per minute—nearly double the rate achieved during the Philippines display, which lasted just over an hour. At that pace, organizers would be launching more than 400 pyrotechnic effects every second throughout the finale. Not everyone is convinced the scale of the display is a good idea. Russell Dickerson, a professor of atmospheric chemistry at the University of Maryland, told Newsweek: "I like fireworks, and a little extra air pollution for a few hours once a year is probably worth it. But 850,000? The air will be full of fine particles and these are linked to major health problems." He added that the particles could contain trace metals including strontium, calcium and magnesium, and warned spectators could face respiratory and cardiovascular effects. While he said N95 masks offer some protection, Dickerson said: "I will not be there nor would I take my grandchildren there." Newsweek reached out to Freedom 250 via email for comment. The fireworks are scheduled to begin at approximately 10:30 p.m. ET on July 4, following remarks by Trump that are scheduled for 9:45 p.m. ET during the Freedom 250 "Salute to America" celebration in Washington, D.C. Fireworks will be launched from the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool, eight barges on the Potomac River, and West Potomac Park. There will be no public fireworks viewing from Capitol Grounds on July 4. Spectators wishing to watch the main display in person are instead being directed to viewing areas along the National Mall, West Potomac Park and surrounding parts of Washington, D.C. Americans who cannot attend in person will be able to watch the broader Salute to America program, which is scheduled to begin at 7 p.m. ET. Fox News has announced extensive live coverage from the National Mall, including a primetime special, "America 250: Celebrating Freedom," airing from 7 p.m. to 11 p.m. ET on July 4. CBS is also set to air "The Great American Block Party 250" live from Washington, D.C., beginning at 8 p.m. ET, with coverage culminating in the fireworks display. Organizers have additionally promoted coverage through Freedom 250''s website and social media channels. On July 3, PBS will broadcast "A Capitol Fourth: 250th Weekend Celebration" live from the West Front of the U.S. Capitol at 8 p.m. ET. Unlike previous years, the annual concert has been moved from July 4 to July 3 for the semi quincentennial celebrations.

entry.title: July 4 Beachgoers Face Risk of Flesh-Eating Bacteria in New York
entry.summary: Ahead of July 4, Americans planning on heading to the shore for the holiday are being urged to take precautions after local officials warned that Vibrio vulnificus, a potentially deadly bacterium often referred to as "flesh-eating bacteria," may be present in coastal waters around New York. The Southampton Town Trustees issued an advisory in April advising residents and visitors that the naturally occurring marine bacterium can be found in warm saltwater and brackish environments during the summer months and that infections, while rare, can quickly become serious in vulnerable individuals. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) says around 1 in 5 people who develop Vibrio vulnificus infection will die due to life-threatening complications, sometimes within a day or two of becoming ill. Millions of Americans could visit beaches during one of the busiest travel weekends of the year. New York has a number of popular beaches and going to the beach is among the most common activities Americans do for the July 4 holiday. R. Sean Norman, a professor and director of the Molecular Microbial Ecology Laboratory in the Department of Environmental Health Sciences at the University of South Carolina, told Newsweek that historically cases of Vibrio vulnificus infections would have been "considered unusual" in New York, as such cases have most commonly been associated with the Gulf Coast and southeastern U.S. However in recent years, "cases this far north are becoming less surprising," he said. "Warmer coastal waters are occurring more frequently in northeastern states, which can create more favorable conditions for Vibrio in places such as bays, estuaries, tidal waters and other brackish coastal environments." "My advice is not to panic, but to take the risk seriously," he added. "These infections are still rare, but Vibrio vulnificus can be very severe." He said to follow the recommended precautions of not swimming in saltwater or brackish water with "open cuts, scrapes, fresh tattoos, piercings, or surgical wounds," and that if exposure is "unavoidable, cover wounds with a waterproof bandage and wash the area thoroughly afterward." The Southampton Town Trustees emphasized that the bacteria''s presence does not indicate pollution or a broader public health emergency, noting that most infections occur when open wounds are exposed to contaminated saltwater or brackish water. People with underlying health conditions or weakened immune systems face the greatest risk and are advised to exercise additional caution. The Southampton advisory is not a beach closure order and does not mean beaches are unsafe for swimming. At the time of publication, Newsweek had not identified any Long Island or New York ocean beaches that had been closed specifically because of Vibrio vulnificus. Newsweek has contacted the New York State Department of Health on Friday morning via email for comment. Popular beaches including Jones Beach, Robert Moses State Park, Fire Island National Seashore, Coopers Beach, Main Beach (East Hampton), Long Beach and Orchard Beach remained subject to routine water quality monitoring rather than Vibrio-specific closures. However, the actual detections from the April Stony Brook study occurred in brackish ponds and bays such as Mecox Bay, Sagaponack Pond, and Georgica Pond. Vibrio vulnificus is a naturally occurring bacterium that thrives in warm coastal and estuarine waters, particularly where saltwater and freshwater mix. People can become infected either by exposing an open wound to contaminated water or by consuming raw or undercooked shellfish, especially oysters. Symptoms of Vibrio vulnificus wound infections can include redness, swelling, severe pain, and skin discoloration. Bloodstream infections can trigger fever, chills, and dangerously low blood pressure. Officials advise anyone experiencing symptoms of wound or bloodstream infection to seek immediate medical care. Those with compromised immune systems—such as those with liver disease, cancer, HIV or receiving immune-suppressing therapy—are more at risk. Antibiotics can be used to treat severe or prolonged Vibrio infections, but for Vibrio wound infections, surgery or amputation can be necessary to remove dead or infected tissue. Health officials have increasingly monitored the bacteria and linked a number of infections in Northeastern states to unusually warm coastal waters, a trend that has continued to prompt warnings from public health agencies and marine scientists. Researchers such as Elizabeth Archer, of the School of Environmental Sciences, University of East Anglia, Norwich, Norfolk, in the U.K., have also said that, with the impacts of climate change, Vibrio vulnificus infections have grown in recent years. The CDC estimates around 100 to 200 Vibrio vulnificus cases are reported each year, though its latest surveillance data goes only as far as 2024, meaning 2025 and 2026 changes are not yet clear.
